deckAnalysis.summary: The deck clearly explains the pressing problem of exam technique over content knowledge, and presents a differentiated AI-based solution with demonstrated MVP. The market sizing is detailed with credible sourcing, and the business model is well defined with plausible monetization and growth metrics. Traction projections are supported by user and MRR goals, though actual current traction is modest and mostly projected. The team section outlines a strong technical founder and a concrete hiring plan. Financial ask is clearly stated with use of funds and valuation. The narrative coherently presents the opportunity and exit strategy. However, some areas lack current customer traction data and detailed team bios beyond founder, which weakens investor confidence. Overall the deck is investor-relevant but would benefit from clearer present traction evidence and deeper team profiles.

recentLinkedInSignals.postText: A big congratulations to Viswa Colluru, PhD and the entire team at Enveda on their Series D announcement by Bloomberg. In the middle of a biotech funding winter, they’ve raised over $300M in less than a year, from some of the most respected partners in the industry and have officially become one of the newest unicorns. There are some founders who make you pause, who pull you into a future that sounds almost too ambitious - until you see how deeply they've thought it through. My first coffee with Viswa Colluru, PhD was just that. He was young, yes, but one of the most wise and long-term thinkers I’d met. He had a 100-year plan to build a new way to discover medicine with a foundational model that would use AI to find and catalog the active ingredients in nature – from plants, to animals, to anything that’s alive basically. Viswa believed that millions of years of evolution could teach us something if we could actually understand the chemistry that current science doesn’t understand. Aspirin, arguably one of the top producing drugs of all time, was in fact discovered by finding the active ingredient (Salicin) in the Willow Tree, which people believed had medicinal abilities. The biggest challenge is the vast chemistry of nature is not known or understood – we know maybe 1% of all life’s chemistry - I know sounds shocking. Even the human blood which has been researched ad nauseam we have cataloged a trivial 15% of. Enveda’s big vision was to unlock the 99% of nature's unknown chemistry with their foundational AI models and the largest searchable databases of life’s chemistry to build one of the best discovery engines for new medicines. Seemed ambitious, but if anyone could figure it out it was him and the incredible team he has put together. 2 years later, they went from a dream to multiple candidates in or entering the clinics and a remarkable pipeline of many hits to follow. We are excited to see this great team continue to execute and push the limits of AI + Chemistry as they build a new way to discover medicines. Wesley Chan and I were compelled to invest from the very beginning after meeting Viswa and are proud to have continued to lead or participate in every round since. Viswa Colluru, PhD, thanks for letting us be a part of your journey –it’s been incredible to see you develop into such a world-class CEO – and remarkably still just as humble as the first day I met you! Also welcome to the board Mikael Dolsten, most recently Chief Scientific Officer of Pfizer. And a big congratulations and thanks to the incredible leadership team - Hannah Gordon, PhD, MSCI, August Allen, Inhwan Kim, Jason Tejani, Dan Wee, PhD, José Trevejo, MD, PhD, Bigyan R. Bista, PhD, and Robert Buckley - who continue to push the limits of science.

Excited to have The Wall Street Journal launch XOPS out of stealth! The first time I met Mayan Mathen, the CEO and co-founder, he didn’t talk about AI. He didn’t rattle off buzzwords or brag about the agentic this & that. He talked about PAIN he and others felt running massive IT orgs! Mayan used to be CTO of NTT (a Fortune 500 company) so when I say he knew the pain, I mean he actually lived it. The kind of pain every CIO knows too well. On top of building new products and tools to push the business forward, they also have to manage the spaghetti soup of software integrations, the unsexy job of keeping everything compliant & secure, ensuring the right access, tickets piling up, tracking assets (datacenters and all) that are often underutilized or even unknown, pulling REAL software usage data, optimizing software management and on and on. Systems strung together with duct tape and yes... even Excel spreadsheets. It’s not sexy so it doesn’t get the love but it’s a massive problem and companies spend a lot of $$$ & people to keep it all together. Mayan wasn’t building a company just to chase a market trend. He was trying to solve the problem that kept him up at night when he was a CIO/CTO. And it hit a chord with me since I knew the problems all too well from my time as CIO. So despite our massive height difference (IYKYK and if you don't picture attached) we just spoke the same language. At the time, he had no customers. Just incredible insight on the problem & how not just to fix it incrementally but fundamentally. I remember thinking: if he’s right, this could completely reshape how the Global 1000 run technology forever. Two years later, XOps is emerging from stealth. They’ve quietly signed some of the largest companies in the world, like Broadcom & Cencora. Reduced staffing needs by over 90%. Taken processes that used to take days and compressed them into minutes. And they’ve done it with a level of customer love that you just don’t see in IT software - sadly a category that has some of the lowest NPS scores out there! While everyone was focused on systems of records, XOPS was quietly building the system of intelligence for the most complex orgs in the world. They aren’t building the tenth agentic AI platform, they are building a platform that gives the Fortune 500 a full system of intelligence so they can truly automate IT ops. This is what it looks like when you build with obsession for the problem, not the hype. I’m also excited to have incredible heavyweights joining Mayan, with Cisco Sanchez, ex-CIO of Qualcomm & FedEx and Parisa Naseralavi, former SVP at ServiceNow - two people who uniquely understand technology and service management at scale. The caliber of leadership at this table speaks volumes to what's next! Wesley Chan and I are thrilled to have led 2 of their rounds. It’s been a joy to work with Mayan, Cisco, Parisa, Brandon Kwong, Andrew Robinson & the entire XOPS team. WSJ exclusive here: bit.ly/4fCbODT

deckAnalysis.summary: The pitch deck presents Standen Capital's strategy to acquire and improve under-managed small recurring-revenue software products with a strict £50,000 all-in acquisition cap. It clearly outlines the problem and solution via operational improvements, identifies a sizable UK SaaS market, details acquisition criteria, sourcing channels, a worked financial example, and a repeatable execution plan with defined operating metrics and risk mitigations. However, the deck lacks detailed information on the founding team, provides no explicit financial ask beyond acquisition cap, and does not disclose current or forecasted actual financials for Standen Capital itself. Narrative is methodical but somewhat dry and technical, insufficiently telling a compelling growth story or investor value proposition beyond operational diligence and risk containment.
strengths: Clear, detailed acquisition criteria and operational improvement process.
Practical approach with hard capital cap limiting downside risk.
Use of metrics and KPIs to manage portfolio performance.
weaknesses: No specific founding team or management details disclosed.
No demonstrated real traction beyond a fictional example.
Limited financial transparency beyond first acquisition capital envelope.
Narrative is dry and lacks compelling investment story and vision.
deckAnalysis.investorReadiness: Moderate. The deck shows a disciplined strategy and process but lacks key elements such as team credibility, demonstrated traction, and full financial clarity, which are critical for early-stage investor confidence.

recentLinkedInSignals.postText: Many investors spend their time looking for the next opportunity. The better question is whether their capital is already positioned to work as effectively as it could. The strongest outcomes I've seen come from disciplined allocation, strong operators, and structures that keep capital productive over time. That matters in any environment, but especially in ones where moving constantly creates noise instead of progress. At Wingfield, that’s how we think about it. Capital should be placed with intention, managed with discipline, and connected to businesses that know how to operate through different cycles. #wingfieldfinancial #privateequity #capitalstrategy #alternativeinvestments #realassets

One thing I’ve learned after decades in lending and real estate is that alignment matters more after every cycle. Who gets paid first. How distributions are structured. How risk is managed before growth is pursued. Those details shape behavior inside a fund. Preferred returns create discipline because they require managers to think carefully about capital deployment, cash flow timing, and downside protection before upside scenarios enter the conversation. A lot of firms talk about aggressive growth. I’ve always been more interested in durable growth, supported by strong fundamentals, operational visibility, and clear incentives across the structure. That matters even more as markets get more complex.

recentLinkedInSignals.postText: There’s an inflection point in the lifecycle of every major market where “what if” shifts to “by when”. This week, a few of these shifts began to occur. What if a power grid could run in orbit? Star Catcher’s $65M Series A says soon. What if e-commerce ran at the speed of compute? Light Anchor (YC P26) just launched on YC. What if AI took scheduling out of human hands entirely? Fastbreak AI is already running schedules across 65+ pro leagues. What if CO₂ became a feedstock instead of a liability? Ohmium and RenewCO₂ are scaling the answer. What’s behind all these shifts? The Claremont Colleges ecosystem. It’s a Claremont Colleges world out there. 👇 https://lnkd.in/gh-3RFxz

Claremont Colleges alumni in New York — we’re hosting a NYC Happy Hour next Tuesday (April 14) and would love to see you there. StoryHouse Ventures is bringing together the Claremont entrepreneurial community for an evening with fellow founders, investors, operators, and executives. We’re co-hosting with Ivan Zinn (CMC ’96), alongside our partners at Rho and Hum Capital. If you’re in the area and want to connect with the Claremont network, details and registration are in the comments. Hope you can make it. Josh Tatum Matthew Estes Blair Silverberg Justin Wolz Drew Parten

recentLinkedInSignals.postText: Will be in Barcelona next week for MWC and 4YFN… It’s been 5 years! Can’t wait ❤️

Congratulations to Ollie Hughes and the entire Count team on launching Count Agent! For builders and Data Teams you are going to ❤️ this: Count has addressed the infrastructure bottleneck that many AI analytics tools face. Typically, data warehouses throttle AI’s speed, but Count has developed a distributed compute layer that intelligently divides work across your warehouse, their servers, and local machines. As a result, 60% of queries now run on users’ browsers, allowing for scalable AI analytics without escalating cloud costs. The canvas approach is innovative. Unlike traditional chat interfaces that create black boxes, Count’s collaborative canvas enables humans and AI to work together transparently, showcasing the work, iterating in real-time, and producing assets that are editable, auditable, and reusable. This eliminates the need for “trust me” outputs. Count is enterprise-ready from launch. With Count Metrics (their semantic layer), version control, and enterprise security, this solution can be deployed across entire organizations. Notably, their biggest agent users are already in the C-Suite, demonstrating the potential to expand beyond just data teams. The technology is so effective that we’ve integrated it throughout our own data stack at TheVentureCity . For investors: This represents a deep infrastructure play with genuine technical moats that can create category-defining companies. The timing is ideal, as data teams have struggled to keep up with demand for decades. That dynamic has now shifted.
